> Obtained results I presented in AACR:

1. **Longer PFS was associated with higher % of arrested cells within
 > each of the compartments**

 a. The differences were the most striking in cancer and t cells
 > (CD4,CD8), this was less noticeable in myeloid and stromal
 > cells

2. **Proliferation state proportions were diverse when comparing short
 > and long pfs**

 a. Lymphocytes post-NACT showed similar patterns to cancer cells.
 > Long PFS showed

3. **Spatial distances**

 a. This part is very interesting

 b. Resting and arrested CD8 T cells were closer to myeloids in
 > postNACT Short-PFS, while proliferative were far away. Similar
 > pattern in Long-PFS but CD8 T cells seemed in general terms
 > more distant to myeloids

4. **Spatial co-ocurrence in between cell-types change in after
 > chemotherapy**

 a. Long PFS seemed to be maintaining many neighbourhoods present in
 > the pre, and showed increased interactions within
 > proliferative cells of all types. Cancer proliferative cells
 > seem to be interacting with many immune cell types that are
 > also proliferating.

 b. Short PFS lost many of those initial interactions seen in Pre,
 > especially within the proliferative compartment (CD4-CD8,
 > CD4-Cancer, CD8-myeloid,CD8-Cancer, CD4-CD8). Also,
 > proliferative myeloid cells lose many of their interactions
 > with Cancer and CD8.

- PAX8 is a positive regulator of TP53 expression in HGSC. However,
 > the oncogenic properties of mutant p53 in HGSC render PAX8 a
 > positive regulator of transformation rather than a growth
 > suppressor. Furthermore, unexpectedly \[28\], the mutant p53
 > upregulates p21 in HGSC, which in this type of cancer plays a
 > non-canonical **cytoplasmic pro-proliferative** role, further
 > enhancing the cancer-promoting effect of
 > PAX8.[[https://www.nature.com/articles/s41388-017-0040-z]{.underline}](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41388-017-0040-z)

- Although the sample was not powered to detect a small difference, we
 > did not observe any difference in p21 staining and localization
 > between previously treated and non-treated tumors, implying that
 > the **ubiquitous p21 cytoplasmic staining was not restricted only
 > to chemotherapy resistant cells.**

**Non-canonic D1 expression**

The nuclear accumulation of cyclin D1 is indicative of a high mitotic
index in cancer cells, and is considered to be a prognostic

esistant cells.**

**Non-canonic D1 expression**

The nuclear accumulation of cyclin D1 is indicative of a high mitotic
index in cancer cells, and is considered to be a prognostic
marker.Cyclin D1 overexpression, as observed in cancer cells,
antagonizes the checkpoint-induced cell cycle arrest, disturbs DNA
replication and allows damaged DNA to be replicated

[Cytoplasmic and membrane-associated cyclin D1 regulates tumor cells
invasion and dissemination]{.underline}.Cytoplasmic cyclin D1 associates
with structural proteins of the cytoskeleton, affecting cell
shape[[https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC6961668/]{.underline}](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC6961668/)

HOW DOES IT WORK: Cyclin D1 expression requires the activation of
RAS-mediated signaling cascades, which is accompanied by
phosphoinositide-3-kinase (PI3K)/AKT-dependent CCND1 translation and a
decrease in cyclin D1 degradation. The active cyclin D1/CDK4/6 complexes
move to the nucleus, and cyclin D1 levels increase from early to late G1
phase, subsequently decreasing during S phase, when cyclin D1 is
exported into the cytoplasm for degradation by the ubiquitin-proteasome
system (UPS). This requires the phosphorylation of the threonine 286
(Figure 1(b)), which is catalyzed by, at least, three kinases: glycogen
synthase-3β (GSK3β), p38 MAP kinase (MAPK) and extracellular

uires the phosphorylation of the threonine 286
(Figure 1(b)), which is catalyzed by, at least, three kinases: glycogen
synthase-3β (GSK3β), p38 MAP kinase (MAPK) and extracellular
signal-regulated kinases (ERK1/2) \[28,29\]. In the cytoplasm, the
ubiquitylation of phosphorylated cyclin D1 is dependent on a SCF complex
consisting of the SKP1 adaptor protein, the CUL1 scaffold protein and a
F-box proteins. Mutations or alterations of cyclin D1-specific E3
ubiquitin ligases contribute to cyclin D1 accumulation in cancers
\[33,34\]. In turn, [the promotion of cyclin D1 degradation suppresses
cancer cell growth]{.underline}. This can be achieved by the knockdown
of USP2 coding a cyclin D1-specific deubiquitylase or by the use of
inhibitors.

Cyclin D1 has a nuclear export signal and is a cargo of exportin 1
(XPO1). However, it has no nuclear localization signal and cannot enter
the nucleus on its own. Instead, its nuclear entry is favored both by
the CIP/KIP (CDK-interacting protein, kinase inhibitory protein) family
members and CDK4, and cyclin D1/CDK4/p21CIP1/p27KIP1 complexes are
imported into the nucleus upon activation

Different cell-cycle signals might be ! in the microenvironment:

ignals might be ! in the microenvironment:

- TME: [M2 macrophages secrete miR-15a and miR-92a to glioma cells
 > through exosomes, and then miR-15a and miR-92a separately bind to
 > CCND1 and RAP1B, thereby blocking the PI3K/AKT/mTOR signaling
 > pathway to inhibit glioma invasion and migration]{.mark}

- 

The Two-Faced Nature of p21 May Depend on its Subcellular Localization:

When in the nucleus, p21 can act as a tumor suppressor: it may inhibit
CKIs in G1 or G2/M cell cycle arrest initiated by p53; it may
downregulate many, but not all, DNA repair pathways; it can limit the
proliferation of stem cells; and it can also induce senescence
[\[10\]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1471491417300205?via%3Dihub#bib0050).

When p21 is concentrated in the cytoplasm, cells with damaged or absent
p53 may acquire oncogenic properties: it may inhibit apoptosis,
facilitate cell migration, and promote cell proliferation

 > cells, but I have also seen eg p21 in stromal-like cells. It
 > would be good to have a tool allowing me to do it, but I guess
 > I need to do it eye-based at the moment.

 - Idea for pixel-based/combination with nuclear masks:

 - If the center pixels of the nuclei have lower value than
 > the pixels on the most external part the staining is
 > most likely cytoplasmic

 - If the center pixels of the nuclei are uniform or higher
 > than the external the staining pattern is most likely
 > nuclear

 - Interesting to see whether these patterns change pre-post NACT
